risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The paper describes the study as a "randomized controlled trial" and refers to a "randomized trial," but it does not report how the allocation sequence was generated. No details such as computer-generated random numbers, random number tables, blocks, stratification, or other explicit random methods are provided. Comment: Because only the label "randomized" is given without context clues about the sequence generation method, the random sequence generation process cannot be judged as truly random.

Summary: The report describes the trial as a randomized controlled trial and gives the interventions, but it does not state any method used to conceal allocation from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or any other concealment mechanism. Randomization alone does not establish concealment, so foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This trial is a three-arm assessor-blinded randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing retro-walking to forward-walking or control." Comment: The study explicitly reports assessor blinding, but does not report blinding of participants or the personnel delivering the walking/physiotherapy interventions. Because the interventions were supervised and obviously different (retro-walking vs forward-walking vs control), participants and therapists would likely have known allocation, and the main outcomes included pain and function, which could plausibly be influenced by that knowledge.

Quote: "This trial is a three-arm assessor-blinded randomized controlled trial (RCT)" and "All measurements will be taken at baseline (week 0), and at the end of the intervention (week 6)." Comment: The study explicitly states that it was assessor-blinded, so the outcome assessor was blinded to allocation. Although pain and WOMAC function are self-reported and could be influenced by participant blinding, the paper describes the trial as assessor-blinded and also uses objective secondary outcomes (hand-held dynamometer, timed up and go test) that are less susceptible to detection bias. Therefore, for the outcome assessment domain, risk of bias is low.

Summary: The provided text describes the trial design and planned 6-week assessments, but it does not report how many participants were randomized, how many were analysed at follow-up, how many were missing, or why any participants withdrew/excluded. Although the review notes 68 participants and the intervention was short, there is no explicit attrition information for this domain, so bias from incomplete outcome data cannot be judged confidently.
